leadership.name: Sanjay Mehrotra
leadership.title: Chairman, President & CEO
leadership.background: Joined Micron May 2017. Previously President & CEO of SanDisk (2011-2016), which he co-founded in 1988 and sold to Western Digital for $19B. Deep NAND/storage background. Leading Micron through the AI memory supercycle with $200B domestic investment commitment.

businessOverview: Micron Technology is the only U.S.-based manufacturer of memory and storage semiconductors, producing DRAM, NAND, and High Bandwidth Memory (HBM) solutions for AI infrastructure, data centers, mobile, and automotive markets.
FY2025 was a record year: $37.4B revenue (+49% YoY), $8.5B GAAP net income — a historic recovery from the FY2023 trough ($15.5B, -49% YoY) driven almost entirely by AI server DRAM and HBM demand.

HBM is the pivotal product: HBM revenue grew ~50% sequentially in Q3 FY2025; entire CY2026 HBM supply is sold out under multi-customer price/volume agreements. In March 2026, Micron entered high-volume production of HBM4 for NVIDIA's Vera Rubin platform.

Micron holds ~21% global HBM market share (2025), up from near-zero in 2023. SK Hynix leads (~52%), Samsung trails. NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang publicly endorsed the Micron partnership — signaling graduation from secondary to strategic supplier.

Only U.S.-based volume memory producer: uniquely positioned to benefit from CHIPS Act funding, customer supply chain diversification, and federal procurement preferences. Announced $200B in domestic capex across Idaho, Virginia, and New York fabs.
brief.productModel: Product sales of DRAM (76.4% of FY2025 revenue), NAND (22.7%), and other (0.9%). Revenue organized across Cloud Memory BU ($13.5B FY2025, +257% YoY), Mobile & Client BU ($11.9B, +2% YoY), and Consumer & Device BU ($7.2B, +45% YoY). Pricing driven by supply/demand dynamics. HBM commands significant ASP premium over standard DRAM — key margin driver. Data center share of global DRAM consumption grew from 32% (5 years ago) to ~50% (2025).

recentNews.headline: Micron Reports Record Q2 FY2026 Revenue of $23.9B; Guides Q3 to $33.5B at ~81% Gross Margin
recentNews.whyItMatters: Revenue nearly tripled YoY — a step-change inflection that validates Micron's HBM positioning. Q3 guidance of $33.5B at 81% gross margin implies FY2026 is tracking to ~$90B+ in revenue, fundamentally re-rating the company's earnings power.

recentNews.headline: Micron First to High-Volume Production of HBM4 for NVIDIA Vera Rubin at GTC 2026
recentNews.whyItMatters: HBM4 delivers 2.8 TB/s bandwidth (2.3x over HBM3E). Being first to high-volume production for NVIDIA's next-gen AI accelerator reduces SK Hynix's near-monopoly risk and cements Micron as a Tier-1 NVIDIA supply chain partner.

recentNews.headline: Micron's Entire CY2026 HBM Capacity Sold Out; Can Only Fulfill ~50-67% of Largest Customer Demand
recentNews.whyItMatters: Demand far outstripping supply through 2026 creates pricing power and revenue visibility typically unseen in commodity memory markets — this structural imbalance is the core bull thesis for sustained margin expansion.

recentNews.headline: Micron Captures ~21% Global HBM Market Share in 2025; Jensen Huang Publicly Endorses Partnership
recentNews.whyItMatters: Public validation from NVIDIA's CEO signals Micron has graduated from a secondary supplier to a strategic partner — crucial competitive moat as HBM becomes the dominant bottleneck in AI compute.

recentNews.headline: Micron Posts Record FY2025 Full-Year Revenue of $37.4B, Up 49% YoY; GAAP Net Income $8.5B
recentNews.whyItMatters: FY2025 marked a historic earnings recovery from the memory downcycle. $8.5B GAAP net income vs. deep losses in FY2023 — HBM and data center DRAM have structurally shifted Micron's margin profile permanently.

recentNews.headline: U.S.-China Trade Truce Reduces Near-Term Export Control Overhang for Micron's China Revenue
recentNews.whyItMatters: China historically represented meaningful Micron DRAM/NAND revenue. White House concession on export control expansion eases a key investor overhang — though SEC filings still flag tariffs and export restrictions as material operating risks.

earningsTakeaways.quarter: FQ3 FY2025 (ended May 29, 2025) — most recently reported

keyMetrics.revenue: $9.30B (FQ3 FY2025); Q4 FY2025 guide: $10.7B; Q2 FY2026 record: $23.9B
keyMetrics.grossMargin: 37.7% GAAP (FQ3 FY2025); guided ~42% in FQ4 FY2025; ~81% guided Q3 FY2026

earningsTakeaways.aiCommentary: CEO Mehrotra: HBM revenue grew nearly 50% sequentially in FQ3 FY2025. Data center revenue more than doubled YoY to a quarterly record. Mass production of 1-gamma DRAM using EUV lithography began ahead of schedule. Micron forecasts HBM TAM growing at ~40% CAGR from ~$35B (2025) to ~$100B (2028). CY2026 HBM supply fully sold out.
earningsTakeaways.demandCommentary: AI training and inference are the dominant demand vectors. Supply tightness expected through and beyond CY2026 due to HBM's conversion-heavy manufacturing process. NAND pricing more volatile than DRAM. Accelerated customer qualification cycles and strong pull-in demand from hyperscalers (Microsoft, Google, Amazon, Meta).

keyRisks: Memory cyclicality: semiconductor memory is notoriously cyclical — FY2023 ($15.5B) vs. FY2025 ($37.4B) illustrates the magnitude of swings. If AI capex decelerates or HBM oversupply emerges post-2026, Micron's revenue and margins could compress significantly

SK Hynix HBM leadership: SK Hynix holds ~52% global HBM market share vs. Micron's ~21% — NVIDIA's longstanding primary HBM supplier relationship with SK Hynix creates concentration risk for Micron's share gain thesis
China export controls: U.S.-China trade tensions and evolving export control regime create binary revenue risk for China-related sales and ongoing uncertainty for capex planning in domestic fabs
Capex intensity: $200B+ domestic investment commitment is massive — if the AI memory cycle turns, Micron could find itself with significant stranded capacity and high fixed costs, as it did in 2023

Samsung competitive response: Samsung is investing heavily in HBM ramp and process node catch-up — a successful Samsung HBM3E/HBM4 recovery could compress Micron's pricing power and market share gains

Customer concentration: NVIDIA and hyperscalers (Microsoft, Google, Amazon) likely represent a significant portion of HBM revenue — loss of a single large HBM customer would be disproportionately impactful

comps.name: SK Hynix
comps.note: #1 HBM market share (~52%). First time exceeded Samsung in operating profit in 2025. Most direct memory comp.

comps.name: Samsung Electronics
comps.note: Global #1 in memory; conglomerate discount compresses multiple. HBM revenue tripled 2025. $233B total revenue.

comps.name: Western Digital
comps.note: NAND/flash storage peer. $9.5B FY2025 revenue. Strong AI data center SSD tailwind.
comps.source: yfinance, close of 2026-06-03 (recomputed: last close × shares + net debt)

comps.name: Seagate
comps.note: $9.1B revenue. HDD storage; AI nearline demand driving growth. $56.7B market cap.
comps.source: yfinance, close of 2026-06-03 (recomputed: last close × shares + net debt)

comps.name: NVIDIA
comps.note: AI infra premium comp. ~$5.3T EV. Primary driver of HBM demand via GPU shipments — upstream demand signal for Micron.
comps.source: yfinance, close of 2026-06-03 (recomputed: last close × shares + net debt)

comps.name: Intel
comps.note: Legacy x86 + foundry turnaround. $52.9B FY2025 revenue. Distressed multiple — contrast to Micron's re-rating illustrates cyclical vs. structural memory bull case.
comps.source: yfinance, close of 2026-06-03 (recomputed: last close × shares + net debt)

slideBullets: Micron is the only U.S.-based volume memory manufacturer — uniquely positioned at the intersection of the AI compute buildout (HBM demand) and domestic supply chain policy (CHIPS Act, $200B capex commitment across Idaho, Virginia, and New York)

FY2025 revenue of $37.4B (+49% YoY) was a record; FY2026 is tracking to ~$90B+ as Q2 FY2026 alone hit $23.9B with Q3 guided to $33.5B at ~81% gross margin — the AI memory supercycle is accelerating, not plateauing

HBM4 in high-volume production for NVIDIA Vera Rubin (March 2026) — Micron was first to ship, reducing SK Hynix's near-monopoly risk and cementing a Tier-1 NVIDIA partnership. Entire CY2026 HBM supply is sold out.

HBM TAM projected to grow at ~40% CAGR from ~$35B (2025) to ~$100B (2028) — Micron's ~21% share gain from near-zero in 2023 is the most significant competitive market share story in semiconductors today

Key risk: memory is highly cyclical (FY2023 revenue of $15.5B, -49% YoY, illustrates the downside). The bull case depends on sustained AI capex from hyperscalers and Micron maintaining HBM market share gains vs. SK Hynix and Samsung
diligenceQuestions: What is Micron's actual HBM revenue as a percentage of total revenue and what are the contractual terms of the CY2026 sold-out supply agreements — specifically, are these fixed-price contracts or does pricing reset, and what is the take-or-pay structure?

How does Micron's HBM yield and cost structure compare to SK Hynix's at the HBM3E and HBM4 nodes? SK Hynix has a multi-year head start — if Micron's yield ramp lags, the gross margin expansion thesis could disappoint even with supply sold out.

What is Micron's exposure to a potential HBM oversupply scenario in 2027-2028 as Samsung recovers and new entrants emerge — and how does the $200B domestic capex commitment constrain Micron's ability to reduce supply if the cycle turns?
